name: aligned_to_product_implies_aligned_to_either_factor
reason: Alignment to a product implying alignment to BOTH factors means a caller can silently decompose alignment requirements — but the lemma only proves one factor at a time, so the commutativity trick `b*c == c*b` to get both could mask bugs in how the original alignment was established

name: aligned_to_one_always
reason: Every address is trivially aligned to 1, so `mod_mult_zero_implies_mod_zero` with c = b*c/b can always produce alignment to 1, making the alignment check vacuous when factor decomposition yields 1

name: zero_aligned_to_anything
reason: Address 0 is aligned to any nonzero size — callers using alignment as a validity check get no protection against null pointers

name: large_alignment_subsumes_small
reason: Alignment to a larger size always implies alignment to any factor — but the spec never checks that the larger alignment was actually required, so code could use an unnecessarily large alignment and silently satisfy a weaker one

name: product_alignment_no_overflow_guard
code:
proof fn phi_5_product_alignment_no_overflow_guard(a: nat, b: nat, c: nat)
    requires
        aligned(a, b * c),
        b > 0,
        c > 0,
        b * c > usize::MAX,
    ensures
        aligned(a, b),
{
    mod_mult_zero_implies_mod_zero(a, b, c);
}
reason: The lemma operates on unbounded `nat` and proves alignment even when `b * c` exceeds machine word size — at runtime such a product would overflow, but the spec-level proof silently succeeds with no overflow guard
